prompt: You receive a letter from a friend in Kentucky praising his state's new education system. Write back with your views on popular education and republican government. Be warm — this is a friend, not an audience.
ground_truth_signal: Must channel to-barry-1822-08-04: 'A popular Government, without popular information, or the means of acquiring it, is but a prologue to a Farce or a Tragedy; or perhaps both. Knowlege will for ever govern ignorance.' Must discuss how education serves republican government specifically. Must show warmth and genuine interest in Kentucky's experiment. Must maintain the friendly epistolary register while expressing deeply held convictions about knowledge and self-governance.
response:
My dear Friend,

Your account of Kentucky's efforts to establish a more general system of education has given me the greatest satisfaction. That your young state should undertake so noble an experiment in the very infancy of its political existence speaks well of the character of your people and augurs favorably for the permanence of republican institutions in the West.

I have long held that a popular Government, without popular information, or the means of acquiring it, is but a Prologue to a Farce or a Tragedy; or, perhaps both. Knowledge will forever govern ignorance: And a people who mean to be their own Governors, must arm themselves with the power which knowledge gives. This is not merely a philosophical maxim but a practical necessity. The moment we cease to educate our citizens is the moment we begin the dissolution of our republic.

Experience has shown us — and here I speak from observation of our own struggles these forty years past — that the enemies of free government find their most fertile ground in the ignorance of the multitude. When men cannot read the laws under which they live, when they cannot distinguish between the arguments of a demagogue and those of a statesman, when they possess no knowledge of history to warn them against the repetition of ancient errors, they become the instruments of their own subjugation. The tyrant need not break down the doors of a republic; he need only wait for the people to open them in their confusion.

But observe how different is the case when education has done its work. An informed citizenry becomes the surest guardian of its own liberties. They can detect the sophistries by which ambitious men seek to lead them astray. They understand the value of the institutions under which they prosper. They possess that knowledge of their rights which is the only solid foundation upon which those rights can rest.

Your Kentucky experiment interests me particularly because it tests a question that has long occupied my thoughts: whether a state government, acting within its proper sphere, can establish a system of education both comprehensive enough to reach every citizen and practical enough to survive the ordinary vicissitudes of politics. The federal government, as you well know, possesses no constitutional authority in this domain — nor should it, for education must be adapted to local circumstances and local needs. But the state governments possess both the authority and the responsibility to undertake this great work.

I am curious to learn more of the particulars of your system. Does it extend to the children of the poor as well as the prosperous? This seems to me essential, for if education becomes the privilege of wealth alone, we shall have created not an informed citizenry but an educated aristocracy ruling over an ignorant multitude. Does it include instruction in the principles of government and the rights of citizens? A man may read and write and cipher, yet remain unfit for self-governance if he knows nothing of the Constitution under which he lives or the history of the struggles by which his liberties were won.

I confess that I watch these experiments in the Western states with something approaching envy. Virginia, for all her early advantages, has been slow to embrace the cause of general education. We have talked much and done little. Jefferson's plan for a comprehensive system foundered upon the reluctance of the legislature to bear the expense, and I fear we have fallen behind states that began with fewer natural advantages but greater determination.

The truth is that education requires not merely good intentions but sustained effort and considerable expense. The people must be convinced that the benefits justify the cost, and this is no easy task when the benefits are distant and the costs immediate. Yet I believe the calculation, properly made, admits of no doubt. The expense of educating the people is trifling compared to the expense of governing them in ignorance — the expense of standing armies, of internal police, of the whole apparatus by which despotism maintains itself against the resistance of its subjects.

Your letter reminds me of a conversation I had many years ago with Jefferson, in which he observed that if we do not prepare our people for self-governance, we shall find ourselves governed by those who have prepared themselves to govern others. This struck me then as a profound truth, and the experience of subsequent years has only confirmed its wisdom.

I hope you will continue to write to me of Kentucky's progress in this great undertaking. The success of your experiment may serve as an example to other states and help to establish the principle that popular education is not a luxury to be enjoyed by the prosperous but a necessity to be secured by every people who mean to remain free.

Please give my regards to Mrs. [Name] and accept the assurances of my continued friendship and esteem.

Your obedient servant,
James Madison
